notes: Kastner served as Marketing Executive in the Corporate Systems group 1987–1988; site of the DECtp launch.
Alternate short-form ID for Digital Equipment Corporation; used in context of DECtp and TPC founding.
Author; Marketing Executive at DEC 1987–1988; ghostwrote Yankee Group report; chaired DECtp Task Force; cofounded TPC membership; later co-founded Aberdeen Group.
Primary competitive target of the DECtp campaign; maker of 3090 mainframe and DB2 database; responded to DEC benchmarks with DB2 Version 2 announcement in October 1988.
Published Kastner's ghostwritten report 'The Future of Transaction Processing' in January 1987.
Kastner's prior six-year employer; provided fault-tolerant computing expertise; later became TPC-A and RAMP-C benchmark audit client.
Kastner left DEC for Aberdeen; established Aberdeen Transaction Services (ATS) as a TPC auditing practice.
Practice area founded by Kastner at Aberdeen Group to provide TPC-certified benchmark auditing; clients included Stratus and Groupe Bull.
Provided the mainframe data center in Westwood, Massachusetts used as the blind benchmark site for the DEC vs. IBM 3090 test.
TPC benchmark audit client for whom Kastner audited DPS 7000 and DPS 6000 systems across the US, Italy, and France.
Cited as the leading x86 server vendor that, by 1990, delivered competitive OLTP throughput at a fraction of DEC VAX pricing.
Founder of Cullinet; previously known to Kastner from his PHI (Prime/PHI) days; built one of the first major commercial software companies.
Former Prime Computer colleague who moved to Yankee Group; hired Kastner to ghostwrite 'The Future of Transaction Processing' for $8,000.
Respected OLTP analyst who had long advocated for an independent benchmark standards body; co-founded TPC with DEC's backing after Kastner's dinner meeting in Los Altos, CA.
Head of DEC's Corporate Systems group; gave Kastner his open-ended assignment to improve DEC's enterprise position; approved Kastner's travel to found TPC.
Head of Competitive Analysis at DEC; used an HP color graphing plotter to produce presentation foils from the benchmark data.
DEC's senior marketing executive; later stated that 'DECtp was our most important launch after VAX.'
Founder of DEC; famously resisted Unix, workstations, and x86 architecture throughout the late 1980s and early 1990s.
IBM researcher; co-inventor of the relational database model with Christopher Date; DB2 designed primarily for query rather than transactional throughput.
IBM researcher; co-inventor of the relational model; DB2's orientation toward query workloads rather than OLTP throughput cited as an architectural limitation.
Earlier employer of Kastner and John Logan; referenced as shared professional background connecting them.
Earlier employer/affiliation of Kastner; context in which he first knew John Cullinane.
Its manual contained a brief chapter on performance auditing that Kastner used as a procedural basis for TPC benchmark auditing.
New DEC group run by Bill Steul that hired Kastner; tasked with improving DEC's enterprise market position.
Trade publication to which Kastner leaked DEC's leadership position in the TPC on August 8, 1988, triggering other vendors to join.
